questions.questionText: The blood supply of the internal capsule is mainly from the:
questions.explanation: The internal capsule is supplied mainly by lenticulostriate branches of the middle cerebral artery (also known as 'arteries of stroke' — Charcot's artery of cerebral hemorrhage). Hemorrhage here causes contralateral hemiplegia. The anterior limb is supplied by medial striate (Heubner's artery from ACA). Posterior limb: lateral striate branches of MCA. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebral Arteries.

questions.questionText: The tract that carries pain and temperature sensation from the body is:
questions.explanation: Lateral spinothalamic tract carries pain and temperature. Anterior spinothalamic carries crude touch and pressure. Dorsal columns (posterior column) carry fine touch, vibration, proprioception, and two-point discrimination. In syringomyelia, the crossing fibers of the lateral spinothalamic tract are first affected → bilateral loss of pain and temperature in a 'cape-like' distribution while preserving dorsal column functions. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spinal Tracts.

questions.questionText: Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of spinal cord) presents with:
questions.optionA: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature
questions.optionB: Contralateral loss of proprioception and vibration
questions.optionC: Ipsilateral motor paralysis and loss of proprioception, contralateral loss of pain and temperature
questions.explanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome (spinal cord hemisection): Ipsilateral — UMN paralysis (corticospinal tract, same side), loss of proprioception/vibration/fine touch (dorsal column, same side). Contralateral — loss of pain and temperature (lateral spinothalamic tract, crosses 1-2 segments above entry). LMN signs at the level of the lesion (ipsilateral). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spinal Cord Lesions.

questions.questionText: The motor area of the cerebral cortex is located in:
questions.explanation: Precentral gyrus (Brodmann area 4) = primary motor cortex. Postcentral gyrus (areas 3,1,2) = primary somatosensory cortex. Broca's area (areas 44,45) = motor speech (inferior frontal gyrus). Wernicke's area (area 22) = receptive speech (superior temporal gyrus). The motor homunculus shows inverted body representation with face laterally and leg medially (extending onto medial surface). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebral Cortex.

questions.questionText: The circle of Willis is formed by all the following arteries EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Circle of Willis: ACA + anterior communicating artery + ICA (terminal part) + posterior communicating artery + PCA + basilar artery (terminal). The MCA is NOT part of the circle — it is a lateral branch of the ICA. The anterior communicating artery is the most common site of Berry aneurysm (causing subarachnoid hemorrhage). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Circle of Willis.

questions.questionText: The most common site of Berry (saccular) aneurysm is:
questions.explanation: Anterior communicating artery is the most common site of Berry aneurysm (~30-35%). Rupture causes subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) — 'thunderclap headache,' worst headache of life, meningism, photophobia. CT scan shows hyperintense blood in subarachnoid space ('star sign'). Lumbar puncture shows xanthochromia. Risk factors: hypertension, ADPKD, Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebrovascular System.

questions.questionText: CSF is produced mainly by the:
questions.explanation: The choroid plexus (present in lateral, 3rd, and 4th ventricles) produces ~500 ml of CSF per day. At any time, ~150 ml is circulating. CSF flows: lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina (of Monro) → 3rd ventricle → cerebral aqueduct (of Sylvius) → 4th ventricle → foramina of Luschka (lateral) and Magendie (median) → subarachnoid space → absorbed by arachnoid granulations into dural venous sinuses. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Ventricular System.

questions.questionText: Communicating hydrocephalus is caused by:
questions.explanation: Communicating hydrocephalus: CSF flows freely through the ventricular system but absorption is impaired (at arachnoid granulations/villi). Causes: post-meningitis, post-SAH, superior sagittal sinus thrombosis. Non-communicating (obstructive): block within the ventricular system (aqueduct stenosis, 4th ventricle tumor, foramen of Monro block). EXAM TRAP: Communicating = absorption problem; Non-communicating = flow obstruction. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — CSF Circulation.

questions.questionText: The cerebellum receives input about conscious proprioception via the:
questions.explanation: Dorsal (posterior) spinocerebellar tract carries unconscious proprioception from the lower limb to the cerebellum (enters via inferior cerebellar peduncle). Ventral (anterior) spinocerebellar tract also carries unconscious proprioception (enters via superior cerebellar peduncle). Corticopontocerebellar tract connects the cerebral cortex to the cerebellum via the pons (largest tract via middle cerebellar peduncle). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebellar Connections.

questions.questionText: The substantia nigra, when degenerated, causes:
questions.explanation: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta causes Parkinson's disease. It leads to dopamine depletion in the striatum → resting tremor ('pill rolling'), rigidity (lead pipe/cogwheel), bradykinesia, and postural instability. Lewy bodies are the histological hallmark. Huntington's disease = caudate nucleus atrophy (loss of GABAergic neurons). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Basal Ganglia.

questions.questionText: The nucleus ambiguus supplies motor fibers to which cranial nerves?
questions.explanation: Nucleus ambiguus (in the medulla) provides motor fibers for CN IX (stylopharyngeus), CN X (pharyngeal and laryngeal muscles), and cranial root of CN XI (to pharynx/larynx). It is the 'special visceral efferent' (SVE) nucleus for these nerves. Damage causes dysphagia, dysphonia, nasal regurgitation. The dorsal motor nucleus of vagus provides parasympathetic fibers. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brainstem Nuclei.

questions.questionText: The tract that decussates at the pyramids of the medulla is:
questions.explanation: The lateral corticospinal tract (pyramidal tract) decussates at the pyramidal decussation in the lower medulla (~85-90% of fibers cross). The remaining 10-15% descend ipsilaterally as the anterior corticospinal tract (crosses at the segmental level). This explains why a lesion above the decussation causes contralateral symptoms, and below causes ipsilateral symptoms. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Motor Pathways.

questions.questionText: Lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome) results from occlusion of:
questions.explanation: Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome) = PICA occlusion (or vertebral artery). Features: ipsilateral — Horner's, cerebellar ataxia, facial pain/temperature loss (spinal nucleus of V), palatal paralysis (nucleus ambiguus), vestibular symptoms. Contralateral — body pain/temperature loss (spinothalamic tract). Notably, NO motor paralysis (pyramids are medial, not lateral). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brainstem Vascular Supply.

questions.questionText: The Edinger-Westphal nucleus provides preganglionic parasympathetic fibers to:
questions.explanation: Edinger-Westphal nucleus (accessory oculomotor nucleus) → preganglionic parasympathetic fibers travel with CN III → synapse in ciliary ganglion → postganglionic fibers via short ciliary nerves → sphincter pupillae (constriction) and ciliary muscle (accommodation). Damage causes mydriasis (dilated pupil) and loss of accommodation. It mediates the pupillary light reflex. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Parasympathetic Nuclei.

questions.questionText: The largest commissural fiber bundle connecting the two cerebral hemispheres is the:
questions.explanation: Corpus callosum is the largest white matter structure and the largest commissure in the brain. Parts (anterior to posterior): rostrum, genu, body, splenium. Agenesis of the corpus callosum is associated with colpocephaly, 'racing car sign' on CT. Split-brain syndrome occurs after callosotomy (used for intractable epilepsy). Anterior commissure connects temporal lobes (olfactory). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebral Commissures.

questions.questionText: The hippocampus is located in which lobe of the brain?
questions.explanation: The hippocampus is located in the medial temporal lobe, forming the floor of the inferior horn of the lateral ventricle. It is crucial for memory consolidation (short-term → long-term). Bilateral hippocampal damage causes anterograde amnesia (e.g., patient H.M.). It is part of the Papez circuit (hippocampus → fornix → mammillary body → anterior thalamic nucleus → cingulate gyrus). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Limbic System.

questions.questionText: A patient has right-sided upper motor neuron facial palsy. The forehead wrinkling is preserved because:
questions.optionA: Forehead muscles have bilateral UMN innervation
questions.optionB: Frontal branch of facial nerve is spared
questions.optionC: Forehead is supplied by trigeminal nerve
questions.optionD: Facial nucleus has no role in forehead
questions.explanation: The upper part of the facial motor nucleus (supplying frontalis and orbicularis oculi) receives bilateral corticobulbar fibers (from both hemispheres). The lower part receives only contralateral fibers. In UMN lesion: contralateral lower face weakness, forehead spared (bilateral innervation). In LMN lesion (Bell's palsy): entire ipsilateral face affected (including forehead). This distinction is key for localization. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Facial Nerve Nuclei.

questions.questionText: The thalamic nucleus involved in relaying pain and temperature sensation to the cortex is:
questions.explanation: VPL nucleus of thalamus relays body somatosensory inputs (pain, temperature, touch, proprioception) to the primary somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus). VPM nucleus relays face sensations (from trigeminal). LGB relays vision. MGB relays auditory. VA and VL nuclei are motor relay nuclei (basal ganglia and cerebellum to motor cortex). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Thalamic Nuclei.

questions.questionText: The blood-brain barrier is formed by:
questions.optionA: Astrocyte foot processes and tight junctions of endothelial cells
questions.explanation: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is formed by: (1) tight junctions (zonulae occludentes) between capillary endothelial cells (the actual physical barrier), (2) basement membrane, (3) astrocyte foot processes (perivascular feet — supporting, not forming the main barrier). Areas WITHOUT BBB: area postrema, pineal gland, posterior pituitary, median eminence, organum vasculosum. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — CNS Barriers.

questions.questionText: The 'area postrema' located in the floor of the 4th ventricle functions as the:
questions.explanation: Area postrema is located in the floor of the 4th ventricle and acts as the chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ). It lacks a blood-brain barrier, allowing it to detect toxins/drugs in the blood. It triggers the vomiting reflex by stimulating the nearby nucleus tractus solitarius. Dopamine D2 receptor antagonists (e.g., metoclopramide) work as antiemetics by blocking D2 receptors here. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brainstem — Fourth Ventricle.

questions.questionText: In a patient with anterior spinal artery syndrome, which modality is PRESERVED?
questions.explanation: Anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior 2/3 of the spinal cord. Occlusion damages corticospinal tracts (bilateral motor paralysis) and spinothalamic tracts (bilateral loss of pain and temperature). The posterior columns (proprioception, vibration, fine touch) are PRESERVED as they are supplied by the posterior spinal arteries. This pattern is called 'dissociated sensory loss.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spinal Cord Blood Supply.

questions.questionText: The middle cerebellar peduncle (brachium pontis) connects the cerebellum to the:
questions.explanation: Three cerebellar peduncles: Superior (brachium conjunctivum) → midbrain (efferent, cerebellar output). Middle (brachium pontis) → pons (afferent, LARGEST peduncle, carries corticopontocerebellar fibers). Inferior (restiform body) → medulla (afferent and efferent, carries spinocerebellar and olivocerebellar tracts). Memory: SIMple = Superior-Inferior-Middle → Midbrain-Medulla-Pons. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cerebellar Peduncles.

questions.questionText: A patient presents with ipsilateral cerebellar signs, contralateral hemiplegia, and ipsilateral CN III palsy. The lesion is in the:
questions.explanation: This describes Weber syndrome (midbrain/cerebral peduncle lesion): ipsilateral CN III palsy (ptosis, dilated pupil, 'down and out' eye) + contralateral hemiplegia (corticospinal tract in crus cerebri). If cerebellar signs are added (involvement of superior cerebellar peduncle), it localizes to the midbrain. Millard-Gubler syndrome = CN VI + VII palsy + contralateral hemiplegia (pontine lesion). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brainstem Syndromes.

questions.questionText: The nerve that passes through the internal acoustic meatus is:
questions.explanation: Internal acoustic meatus transmits: CN VII (facial), CN VIII (vestibulocochlear), nervus intermedius (sensory root of VII), and labyrinthine artery (branch of AICA, rarely basilar). Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma) arises in this canal, compressing CN VIII first (sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus) and later CN VII (facial weakness) and CN V (loss of corneal reflex). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Skull Base.

questions.questionText: The spinal cord terminates (conus medullaris) in adults at the level of:
questions.explanation: In adults, the spinal cord (conus medullaris) terminates at L1-L2 level. In neonates, it ends at L3. Below the conus, the cauda equina (nerve roots) continues. Lumbar puncture is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5 (below the conus to avoid spinal cord damage). The filum terminale (pia mater extension) continues from the conus to the coccyx. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spinal Cord.

questions.questionText: The dural venous sinus that lies in the attached border of the falx cerebri is:
questions.explanation: Superior sagittal sinus runs in the attached (upper) border of the falx cerebri, from the crista galli to the internal occipital protuberance (confluence of sinuses/torcular Herophili). It receives the superior cerebral veins and arachnoid granulations (CSF absorption). Inferior sagittal sinus runs in the free (lower) border of the falx. Straight sinus runs at the junction of falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Dural Sinuses.

questions.questionText: The center for conjugate lateral gaze is located in:
questions.explanation: PPRF (paramedian pontine reticular formation) is the brainstem center for horizontal conjugate gaze. It coordinates ipsilateral lateral rectus (CN VI) and contralateral medial rectus (CN III via MLF) for conjugate lateral eye movement. Damage to PPRF causes loss of ipsilateral horizontal gaze. Frontal eye field (area 8) is the cortical center — stimulation causes contralateral gaze. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Eye Movement Control.

questions.questionText: Which structure is located in the floor of the fourth ventricle?
questions.explanation: The floor of the 4th ventricle (rhomboid fossa) contains several important structures: facial colliculus (elevation formed by abducens nucleus with facial nerve fibers looping over it), vagal trigone, hypoglossal trigone, vestibular area, striae medullares, area postrema. The pineal gland is in the posterior wall of the third ventricle. Mamillary bodies are in the floor of the third ventricle. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Fourth Ventricle.

questions.questionText: Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (due to Vitamin B12 deficiency) affects:
questions.optionA: Lateral corticospinal tract and dorsal columns
questions.explanation: Subacute combined degeneration (vitamin B12 deficiency) affects: (1) Dorsal (posterior) columns → loss of proprioception, vibration, positive Romberg's sign, sensory ataxia, (2) Lateral corticospinal tracts → UMN signs (spasticity, Babinski positive). 'Combined' = combined posterior + lateral column disease. Unlike tabes dorsalis (syphilis) which only affects dorsal columns. Peripheral neuropathy may also coexist. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Spinal Cord Pathology.
